message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "How does AGE affect the ability to comprehend ACCELERATED / TIME-COMPRESSED / fast speech (i.e. "speed listening")? Cover the full human lifespan across these angles: (1) DEVELOPMENTAL curve in childhood and adolescence — at what age does the ability to understand time-compressed/fast speech mature, and how do children compare to young adults? (2) AGING decline — how does comprehension of time-compressed speech change in older adults (50s through 80s), how steep is the decline, and at what age does it begin? Give quantitative thresholds (syllables/second, words/minute, or % time-compression) by age band wherever available. (3) MECHANISMS — disentangle peripheral hearing loss (presbycusis) from central/cognitive factors: central auditory temporal processing, cognitive/perceptual processing speed, and working-memory / phonological-loop capacity; which factor dominates the age effect, and does the age penalty persist after controlling for audibility? (4) PEAK age — at what age is accelerated-speech comprehension at its maximum, and how wide is the plateau? (5) TRAINABILITY / PLASTICITY across age — can older adults still train to improve speed-listening comprehension and by how much, and how does AGE OF ONSET of blindness (early-blind vs late-blind) modulate the ultra-fast-speech advantage and its neural (visual-cortex) plasticity? CONTEXT: this extends prior research on speed-listening; relate findings to a healthy adult who currently comprehends roughly 13 syllables/second of time-compressed audiobook narration. Prefer peer-reviewed sources; provide quantitative figures and effect sizes by age group where possible, and flag where peripheral hearing loss is or isn't controlled for."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/aging-neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnagi.2022.887581/full
**Title:** The recognition of time-compressed speech as a function of age in listeners with cochlear implants or normal hearing (Frontiers in Aging Neuroscience, 2022)
**Found via:** Quantitative thresholds: syllables/sec, WPM, % compression by age band search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the source content first.

input.prompt:
Extract findings about how AGE affects recognition/comprehension of time-compressed (fast) speech. Specifically:
1. Quantitative thresholds by age band: syllables/sec, words/minute, or % time-compression. What compression rates were tested?
2. The developmental/aging decline curve: at what age does comprehension of time-compressed speech start to decline, and how steep?
3. Mechanisms: peripheral hearing loss (presbycusis) vs central/cognitive factors (temporal processing, processing speed, working memory). Does the age penalty persist after controlling for audibility/hearing?
4. Comparison between normal-hearing listeners and cochlear implant users.
5. Any effect sizes, correlation coefficients, regression slopes, or statistics relating age to time-compressed speech recognition.
6. The publication date and authors.
Quote exact sentences/statistics from the paper where possible.

content.content:
# Age Effects on Time-Compressed Speech Recognition

**Publication:** Tinnemore et al., *Frontiers in Aging Neuroscience*, September 29, 2022

## 1. Compression Rates Tested

Four degrees of time compression were employed:
- 0% (baseline): ~3.7 syllables/second
- 20%: ~4.6 syllables/second
- 40%: ~6.4 syllables/second
- 60%: ~10 syllables/second

## 2. Age-Related Decline Curve

The study divided participants into three age groups:
- Younger: 19–23 years (NH); 20–48 years (CI users)
- Middle-aged: 52–64 years (NH); 50–63 years (CI users)
- Older: 65–78 years (NH); 65–82 years (CI users)

**Key finding:** "Performance was at ceiling for unprocessed speech in the 0 and 20% time-compressed conditions and declined beginning at 40% time compression." Critically, middle-aged and older listeners showed similar deficits: "the performance of the middle-aged and older groups is plotted as a difference...performance of the younger group" with "larger" declines at 40–60% compression.

## 3. Mechanisms: Peripheral vs. Central Factors

**Central/cognitive dominance:** The authors concluded that "the deficits in speech recognition of older listeners for time-compressed speech may be primarily affected by age-related declines in central auditory processing rather than solely related to peripheral age-related hearing loss."

**Processing speed hypothesis rejected:** Contrary to expectations, "cognitive processing speed did not predict recognition of time-compressed speech," suggesting general processing speed is not the mechanism.

The study design specifically tested this by comparing normal-hearing listeners to cochlear implant users (whose devices bypass much peripheral pathology): "If the documented age-related deficit for recognizing time-compressed speech is primarily a result of cochlear hearing loss...a comparison between younger and older listeners with CIs would not show an age-related deficit."

**Result:** Both groups showed similar age-related patterns, supporting central mechanisms.

## 4. CI vs. Normal-Hearing Comparison

"Results from the generalized linear mixed effects model comparing the performance of the two groups...show a main effect of listener group (ListenerGroupCI, z = −7.05, p < 0.001), with listeners with CIs generally performing more poorly than listeners with NH presented with vocoded speech."

However, the critical finding was: "There were no significant listener group × age group interactions (all p's > 0.05). This lack of an interaction between listener group and age group suggests that the age-related differences in recognition of time-compressed speech...may be similar in both CI and NH listener groups."

## 5. Effect Sizes and Statistics

For older listeners with normal hearing at 60% compression vs. younger:
- AgeMNH × TC60: log-odds estimate = −0.96, z = −8.27, p < 0.001
- AgeONH × TC60: log-odds estimate = −0.95, z = −7.91, p < 0.001

For cochlear implant users at 40% compression:
- AgeMCI × TC40: z = −3.45, p < 0.001
- AgeOCI × TC40: z = −4.27, p < 0.001

The authors note: "The older and middle age groups showed similar performances, regardless of the mode of listening...indicating that potential age-related differences in central auditory processing may affect performance by adults prior to 65 years of age."

content.text: Based on the fetched content, I have sufficient information to extract the key claims. This is a peer-reviewed primary research article from Frontiers in Aging Neuroscience.

claims.claim: Time-compressed speech recognition is at ceiling for younger normal-hearing listeners at 0% and 20% compression but declines starting at 40% compression; the four compression levels corresponded to approximately 3.7, 4.6, 6.4, and 10 syllables/second (0%, 20%, 40%, 60% compression respectively).
claims.quote: Performance was at ceiling for unprocessed speech in the 0 and 20% time-compressed conditions and declined beginning at 40% time compression.

claims.claim: Age-related deficits in recognizing time-compressed speech appear before age 65: middle-aged (50s-60s) and older (65+) groups showed similar deficits relative to younger listeners, with the largest declines at 40-60% compression.
claims.quote: The older and middle age groups showed similar performances, regardless of the mode of listening...indicating that potential age-related differences in central auditory processing may affect performance by adults prior to 65 years of age.

claims.claim: The age penalty for time-compressed speech persists after controlling for peripheral hearing, because cochlear-implant users (who bypass much peripheral cochlear pathology) showed the same age-related decline as normal-hearing listeners; thus the deficit is attributed primarily to central auditory processing rather than presbycusis.
claims.quote: the deficits in speech recognition of older listeners for time-compressed speech may be primarily affected by age-related declines in central auditory processing rather than solely related to peripheral age-related hearing loss.

claims.claim: General cognitive processing speed did NOT predict recognition of time-compressed speech, arguing against a general processing-speed mechanism for the age effect.
claims.quote: cognitive processing speed did not predict recognition of time-compressed speech

claims.claim: There was no significant listener-group (CI vs NH) by age-group interaction (all p's > 0.05), indicating age-related differences in time-compressed speech recognition are similar in both CI and NH listeners; quantitatively, older NH listeners at 60% compression had log-odds estimate -0.96 (z = -8.27, p < 0.001) relative to younger.
claims.quote: There were no significant listener group × age group interactions (all p's > 0.05). This lack of an interaction between listener group and age group suggests that the age-related differences in recognition of time-compressed speech...may be similar in both CI and NH listener groups.

content.text: Extracted 5 falsifiable claims from the Tinnemore et al. (2022) Frontiers in Aging Neuroscience primary research article. Key findings relevant to the research question: time-compressed speech recognition declines starting at 40% compression (~6.4 syll/sec), age deficits emerge before 65 (middle-aged listeners already affected), and the age penalty persists after controlling for peripheral hearing because cochlear-implant users show the same decline as normal-hearing listeners, pointing to central auditory processing rather than presbycusis as the dominant mechanism. Notably, general cognitive processing speed did NOT predict performance.
